article.title: Transcription factor StWRKY1 regulates phenylpropanoid metabolites conferring late blight resistance in potato
article.license: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0/), which permits unrestricted reuse, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited.

events.event_label: sHSP17.8 expression increased in resistant potato during late blight infection
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p078.s001: We detected significantly high fold changes of sHSP17.8 (fold change=40.35) in a resistant genotype of potato against late blight infection. PMC4765800.p078.s002: The presence of HSEs on the promoter region of the StWRKY1 gene in the resistant genotype, but not in the susceptible genotype (Supplementary Table S4), helped the sHSPs to interact and induce the transcription factor StWRKY1. PMC4765800.p078.s003: Small HSPs interact and provide stability to the proteins by acting as molecular chaperones. PMC4765800.p078.s004: LUC assay confirmed the transcriptional regulation of StWRKY1 by sHSP17.8.

events.event_label: pathogen infection upregulates 4-CL, THT and TyDC
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p080.s001: To combat pathogen infection, plants have developed specific metabolic pathways to biosynthesize metabolites that reinforce secondary cell walls as mechanical barriers. PMC4765800.p080.s002: Following pathogen inoculation, genes involved in the production of tyramine-derived HCAAs were 4-CL, THT, and TyDC, which were upregulated 2.13-, 2.98-, and 4.04-fold, respectively, in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p080.s003: Concurrently, metabolites produced from these candidate RRI genes, i.e. N-feruloyltyramine and N-feruloyloctopamine, were induced with significantly high fold changes in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p080.s004: The authors previous studies reported high-fold-change expression of THT and TyDC genes, along with high-fold-change accumulation of RRI metabolites N-feruloyltyramine, N-caffeoyltyramine, and N-feruloyloctopamine in leaves of resistant genotypes.

events.event_label: Transcription factors affect RR genes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p051.s003: Deposition of HCAAs (blue fluorescence intensity) was greater in pathogen-treated resistant than in mock-treated resistant and pathogen- or mock-treated susceptible genotypes (Supplementary Fig. S4 at JXB online). PMC4765800.p051.s004: The candidate RRI metabolites with the highest fold changes in abundance following pathogen inoculation were mapped onto their metabolic pathways (Fig. 2) to identify the catalytic enzymes, which were then searched in the Spud database ( accessed on 25 February 2015) to identify candidate RR genes. PMC4765800.p051.s005: The RR genes that encoded the enzymes of these RRI metabolites were 4-coumarate:CoA ligase (4-CL) (GenBank accession number AF150686.1; chromosome 3), hydroxycinnamoyl transferase (HCT) (Gene ID: PGSC0003DMT400036695; chromosome 3), tyramine hydroxycinnamoyl transferase (THT) (GenBank accession number JX896425.1; chromosome 10), and tyrosine decarboxylase (TyDC) (Gene ID: PGSC0003DMT400038501; chromosome 3) (Fig. 2). PMC4765800.p051.s006: The expression of RR genes is transcriptionally regulated by the co-ordinated action of transcription factors which bind to cis-acting elements and regulate the expression of RR genes. PMC4765800.p051.s007: Previous studies reported that StWRKY1 (GenBank accession number AJ278507.1; chromosome 5) is induced and co-regulated with class I endochitinase during the compatible interaction of potato and P. infestans.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 enhances late blight resistance and affects secondary cell walls
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p067.s006: However, no significant reduction was observed in HCT transcripts in silenced plants (fold change=2.22) compared with resistant plants (2.41) and this was probably due to the absence of the W1-box in the HCT promoter (Fig. 6G and Supplementary Table S3). PMC4765800.p067.s007: We then tested the effect of StWRKY1 silencing on the production of HCAAs. PMC4765800.p067.s008: The RRI metabolites N-feruloyltyramine (fold change=10.13), N-feruloyloctopamine (6.65), feruloylputrescine (3.80), and feruloylagmatine (3.44) were reduced (P<0.05) after silencing (Table 2). PMC4765800.p067.s009: Taken together, these results provide compelling evidence that StWRKY1 is a positive regulator of late blight resistance through the reinforcement of secondary cell walls.

events.event_label: sHSPs interact with and activate StWRKY1
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p078.s001: We detected significantly high fold changes of sHSP17.8 (fold change=40.35) in a resistant genotype of potato against late blight infection. PMC4765800.p078.s002: The presence of HSEs on the promoter region of the StWRKY1 gene in the resistant genotype, but not in the susceptible genotype (Supplementary Table S4), helped the sHSPs to interact and induce the transcription factor StWRKY1. PMC4765800.p078.s003: Small HSPs interact and provide stability to the proteins by acting as molecular chaperones. PMC4765800.p078.s004: LUC assay confirmed the transcriptional regulation of StWRKY1 by sHSP17.8.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 induces HCAA biosynthetic genes and secondary cell wall thickening
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p080.s006: The results of the EMSA and LUC assays confirmed that StWRKY1 physically interacts with and induces high-fold-change expression of downstream genes involved in secondary cell wall thickening. PMC4765800.p080.s007: Likewise, the overexpression of VvWRKY1 induced a cinnamyl alcohol dehydrogenase and a caffeic acid O-methyl transferase gene in grape vines, providing higher resistance to downy mildew. PMC4765800.p080.s008: Taken together, these findings suggest that StWRKY1 takes part in a regulatory network, directly controlling secondary cell wall thickening by acting on promoters of HCAA biosynthetic genes.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 regulation of phenylpropanoid metabolites contributes to late blight resistance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p001.s001: Transcription factor StWRKY1 regulates phenylpropanoid metabolites conferring late blight resistance in potato

events.event_label: Phenylpropanoid metabolites in stems enhance cell wall thickening, contributing to late blight resistance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p009.s003: The two genotypes with contrasting levels of resistance to Phytophthora infestans induced different metabolic pathways. PMC4765800.p009.s004: As reported by others, a high fold change in abundance of phenylpropanoid metabolites was observed in stems that appears to play a major role in late blight resistance by enhancing cell wall thickening through deposition of polyaromatic domains of suberin. PMC4765800.p009.s005: Phenylpropanoids were also observed in the current authors’ previous studies, but the molecular events in their biosynthesis and regulation were not revealed. PMC4765800.p009.s006: A hierarchy of genes involved in the induction of the phenylpropanoid pathway has been identified, although this is limited to model plants. PMC4765800.p009.s007: Therefore, candidate metabolites with high fold change in abundances were mapped onto metabolic pathways to identify their biosynthetic candidate genes based on genomic databases.

events.event_label: Infection of potato by P. infestans induces expression of StWRKY1 in potato
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p074.s001: Induction of StWRKY1 in potato against P. infestans infection

events.event_label: phospholipid metabolites activate MAPK cascade and oxidative bursts in defence signalling
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p076.s001: We also looked into the signalling pathways which involved induction of the WRKY transcription factor and detected high fold changes in several induced phospholipid metabolites in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p076.s002: These phospholipids act as novel secondary messengers in plant defence signalling pathways and activate the MAPK cascade by triggering oxidative bursts. PMC4765800.p076.s003: MAPK converts the signals generated from receptors to cellular responses. PMC4765800.p076.s004: Upon activation, MAPK translocates into the nucleus and phosphorylates the WRKY transcription factor, which in turn activates downstream gene expression.

PMC4765800.p080.s005: However, here, for the first time, StWRKY1 was associated with the promoters of downstream HCAA biosynthetic genes 4-CL and THT in vivo and in vitro. PMC4765800.p080.s006: The results of the EMSA and LUC assays confirmed that StWRKY1 physically interacts with and induces high-fold-change expression of downstream genes involved in secondary cell wall thickening. PMC4765800.p080.s007: Likewise, the overexpression of VvWRKY1 induced a cinnamyl alcohol dehydrogenase and a caffeic acid O-methyl transferase gene in grape vines, providing higher resistance to downy mildew.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 silencing reduces abundance of N-feruloyltyramine, N-feruloyloctopamine, feruloylputrescine, and feruloylagmatine
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p067.s006: However, no significant reduction was observed in HCT transcripts in silenced plants (fold change=2.22) compared with resistant plants (2.41) and this was probably due to the absence of the W1-box in the HCT promoter (Fig. 6G and Supplementary Table S3). PMC4765800.p067.s007: We then tested the effect of StWRKY1 silencing on the production of HCAAs. PMC4765800.p067.s008: The RRI metabolites N-feruloyltyramine (fold change=10.13), N-feruloyloctopamine (6.65), feruloylputrescine (3.80), and feruloylagmatine (3.44) were reduced (P<0.05) after silencing (Table 2). PMC4765800.p067.s009: Taken together, these results provide compelling evidence that StWRKY1 is a positive regulator of late blight resistance through the reinforcement of secondary cell walls.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 activates HCAA biosynthetic genes and metabolites
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p084.s003: Concurrently, sHSP17.8 is also induced in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p084.s004: sHSP17.8 interacts with HSEs present in the StWRKY1 promoter region to enable functioning of StWRKY1. PMC4765800.p084.s005: Consequently, StWRKY1 protein interacts with HCAA biosynthetic genes 4-CL, TyDC, THT, and possibly others, inducing high-fold-change production of the HCAA metabolites N-feruloyltyramine, N-feruloyloctopamine, and others. PMC4765800.p084.s006: Taken together, these results suggest that StWRKY1 is required for the induction of HCAAs and defence by secondary cell wall thickening in potato against P. infestans. PMC4765800.p084.s007: These candidate genes can be used to replace non-functional genes in susceptible commercial cultivars, e.g. Shepody and Russet Burbank, based on genome-editing technologies to enhance quantitative resistance.

events.event_label: Pathogen infection increases abundance of (3′-sulfo)Galβ-Cer, N-palmitoylsphingosine, N-(eicosanoyl)-sphinganine, 1-(9Z-nonadecenoyl)-glycero-3-phosphoserine, 1-hexadecyl-2-propionyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine, and 1-octadecyl-2-ethyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p059.s001: We detected a high fold change in abundance of induced phospholipid metabolites in the resistant genotype following pathogen infection [sphingolipids: (3′-sulfo)Galβ-Cer(d18:0/20:0(2OH)) (3.77), N-palmitoylsphingosine (2.90), and N-(eicosanoyl)-sphinganine (2.37); glycerophospholipids: 1-(9Z-nonadecenoyl)-glycero-3-phosphoserine (3.61), 1-hexadecyl-2-propionyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.50), and 1-octadecyl-2-ethyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.14)]. PMC4765800.p059.s002: These phospholipids are structural components of membranes and act as novel secondary messengers in plant defence signalling pathways. PMC4765800.p059.s003: These activate the MAPK cascade by triggering oxidative bursts. PMC4765800.p059.s004: MAPK (StMEK1) phosphorylates StWRKY1, which in turn activates defence gene expression.

events.event_label: PtoWRKY60 upregulates defence genes PR5.1, PR5.2, PR5.4, PR5.5 and CPR5
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p075.s006: Likewise, the overexpression of CaWRKY27 in tobacco plants was associated with resistance to Ralstonia solanacearum infection through induction of jasmonic acid-, salicylic acid-, and ethylene-mediated signalling pathways. PMC4765800.p075.s007: Resistance to poplar canker was associated with overexpression of PtoWRKY60, which regulated high expression of defence-associated genes, such as PR5.1, PR5.2, PR5.4, PR5.5, and CPR5, in poplar. PMC4765800.p075.s008: Two CaWRKY1 genes, CaWRKY1a and CaWRKY1b (which bind to the pW1a promoter region), were identified in Coffea arabica and conferred resistance to the rust fungus Hemileia vastatrix. PMC4765800.p075.s009: These findings depict the involvement of the WRKY transcription factors in disease resistance, as described here in potato against P. infestans.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 promotes HCAA accumulation and secondary cell wall thickening
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p084.s003: Concurrently, sHSP17.8 is also induced in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p084.s004: sHSP17.8 interacts with HSEs present in the StWRKY1 promoter region to enable functioning of StWRKY1. PMC4765800.p084.s005: Consequently, StWRKY1 protein interacts with HCAA biosynthetic genes 4-CL, TyDC, THT, and possibly others, inducing high-fold-change production of the HCAA metabolites N-feruloyltyramine, N-feruloyloctopamine, and others. PMC4765800.p084.s006: Taken together, these results suggest that StWRKY1 is required for the induction of HCAAs and defence by secondary cell wall thickening in potato against P. infestans. PMC4765800.p084.s007: These candidate genes can be used to replace non-functional genes in susceptible commercial cultivars, e.g. Shepody and Russet Burbank, based on genome-editing technologies to enhance quantitative resistance.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 contributes to secondary cell wall thickening and late blight resistance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p003.s001: StWRKY1 transcription factor mediates secondary cell wall thickening in potato by regulating expression of tyramine-related hydroxycinnamic acid amide biosynthetic genes, thus contributing to resistance to late blight disease.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 induces HCAAs which accumulate in secondary cell walls
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p004.s009: Silencing of the StWRKY1 gene was associated with signs of reduced late blight resistance by significantly increasing the pathogen biomass and decreasing the abundance of HCAAs. PMC4765800.p004.s010: This study provides convincing evidence on the role of StWRKY1 in the regulation of downstream genes to biosynthesize HCAAs, which are deposited to reinforce secondary cell walls.

events.event_label: WRKY overexpression confers tolerance to pathogen infection by inducing phytohormone-mediated signalling pathways
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p008.s001: WRKY domain-containing proteins are one of the largest families of transcriptional regulators in plants. PMC4765800.p008.s002: They have a DNA-binding domain called a W-box (TTGACC/T or WRKYGQK) and are involved in plant immune responses. PMC4765800.p008.s003: Overexpression of WRKY transcription factor increased tolerance to pathogen infection through induction of phytohormone-mediated signalling pathways in grapes and tobacco. PMC4765800.p008.s004: In contrast, the overexpression of WRKY reduced resistance mechanisms in rice and Capsicum plants.

events.event_label: StMEK1 and StWRKY1 induce RR genes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p076.s003: MAPK converts the signals generated from receptors to cellular responses. PMC4765800.p076.s004: Upon activation, MAPK translocates into the nucleus and phosphorylates the WRKY transcription factor, which in turn activates downstream gene expression. PMC4765800.p076.s005: In potato, StMEK1 was functionally characterised during defence reactions, which are also associated with StWRKY1, as was confirmed here by BiFC assay. PMC4765800.p076.s006: StMEK1 and StWRKY1 function together to induce the expression of downstream RR genes involved in plant defence.

events.event_label: sHSP RSI2 expressed in tomato interacts with R protein I-2
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p078.s006: The HSE present in the promoter region of the Arabidopsis APX1 gene acts as a functional component that helps to protect plants from oxidative stress. PMC4765800.p078.s007: In tobacco, the sHSP Ntshsp17 acts as a molecular chaperone and helps in the maintenance of cellular conditions suitable for inducing defence responses against bacterial wilt pathogen Ralstonia solanacearum by stabilizing signalling-related proteins. PMC4765800.p078.s008: In tomato, the sHSP RSI2 is a HSP20 member that specifically interacts and provides stability to the Fusarium oxysporum R protein I-2. PMC4765800.p078.s009: Likewise, the induction of mitochondrial HSP22 occurred in tomato during oxidative stress and helped to provide adoptive responses. PMC4765800.p078.s010: Conflicting with these results, wheat sHSP, Mds1 present on chromosome 3AS increases susceptibility to wheat gall midge and powdery mildew fungus Blumeria graminis f. sp. tritici.

events.event_label: VvWRKY1 induces cinnamyl alcohol dehydrogenase and caffeic acid O-methyl transferase
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p080.s006: The results of the EMSA and LUC assays confirmed that StWRKY1 physically interacts with and induces high-fold-change expression of downstream genes involved in secondary cell wall thickening. PMC4765800.p080.s007: Likewise, the overexpression of VvWRKY1 induced a cinnamyl alcohol dehydrogenase and a caffeic acid O-methyl transferase gene in grape vines, providing higher resistance to downy mildew. PMC4765800.p080.s008: Taken together, these findings suggest that StWRKY1 takes part in a regulatory network, directly controlling secondary cell wall thickening by acting on promoters of HCAA biosynthetic genes.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 and HCAA biosynthetic genes contribute to secondary cell wall thickening
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p082.s001: To further assess the effect of StWRKY1 on the resistance mechanisms, StWRKY1 was silenced in a resistant genotype, and silenced and non-silenced resistant plants were compared based on relative gene expression of HCAA biosynthetic genes, accumulation of their downstream biosynthesized RRI metabolites, amount of fungal biomass, and disease severity. PMC4765800.p082.s002: The transcript abundance of StWRKY1 and its regulating RRI genes, 4-CL, THT, and TyDC, was reduced (P<0.05) in silenced plants (fold change=1.16, 0.40, 0.44, and 0.71, respectively) compared with resistant plants (5.52, 2.13, 2.98, and 4.04, respectively). PMC4765800.p082.s003: However, no significant reduction was observed for HCT transcripts, probably due to the absence of the W1-box. PMC4765800.p082.s004: The abundance of the HCAA metabolites N-feruloyltyramine and N-feruloyloctopamine was reduced 2-fold after silencing, resulting in a higher (P<0.001) biomass in silenced plants (fold change=13.06).

events.event_label: Pathogen infection induces heat shock protein and StWRKY1
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p004.s006: Sequencing of the promoter region of StWRKY1 in susceptible genotypes revealed the absence of heat shock elements (HSEs). PMC4765800.p004.s007: Simultaneous induction of both the heat shock protein (sHSP17.8) and StWRKY1 following pathogen invasion enables functioning of the latter to interact with the HSE present in the resistant StWRKY1 promoter region. PMC4765800.p004.s008: EMSA and luciferase transient expression assays further revealed direct binding of StWRKY1 to promoters of hydroxycinnamic acid amide (HCAA) biosynthetic genes encoding 4-coumarate:CoA ligase and tyramine hydroxycinnamoyl transferase. PMC4765800.p004.s009: Silencing of the StWRKY1 gene was associated with signs of reduced late blight resistance by significantly increasing the pathogen biomass and decreasing the abundance of HCAAs. PMC4765800.p004.s010: This study provides convincing evidence on the role of StWRKY1 in the regulation of downstream genes to biosynthesize HCAAs, which are deposited to reinforce secondary cell walls.

events.event_label: Pathogen infection induces higher abundance of fatty acids, alkaloids, and phenylpropanoids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p050.s003: Thirty-four of the identified metabolites were phenylpropanoids, including the HCAAs N-feruloyltyramine, N-feruloyloctopamine, feruloylputrescine, and feruloylagmatine. PMC4765800.p050.s004: In addition to phenylpropanoids, 24 fatty acids and four alkaloids were induced in higher abundance in the resistant than in the susceptible genotype following pathogen inoculation.

events.event_label: Phytohormones (jasmonic acid, ethylene, abscisic acid, gibberellins) confer tolerance or reduce susceptibility to pathogen
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p007.s003: To combat PTI, pathogens produce haustoria inside the plant cell to release effector proteins. PMC4765800.p007.s004: This invokes a second line of defence in plants to produce effector-specific resistance (R) proteins encoded by R genes. PMC4765800.p007.s005: The resulting hypersensitive reaction or effector-triggered immunity (ETI) constitutes qualitative resistance. PMC4765800.p007.s006: PAMP also triggers phytohormones, such as jasmonic acid, salicylic acid, ethylene, abscisic acid, and gibberellins, to provide broad biochemical resistance and reduce pathogen growth. PMC4765800.p007.s007: These phytohormones fine-tune the pathways involved in the production of secondary metabolites that impart quantitative resistance.

events.event_label: Pathogen detected in stems is associated with disease severity
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p043.s001: Disease progress curve and pathogen biomass in infected stems of resistant (F06025) and susceptible (Shepody) genotypes inoculated with P. infestans: (A) Late blight disease severity progress curve based on lesion length (mm) and (B) biomass of pathogen quantified as relative P. infestans-specific (O-8) gene expression at 6 dpi. PMC4765800.p043.s002: Significant differences between susceptible and resistant plants using Student’s t-test: *P<0.05; **P<0.01.

events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p004.s006: Sequencing of the promoter region of StWRKY1 in susceptible genotypes revealed the absence of heat shock elements (HSEs). PMC4765800.p004.s007: Simultaneous induction of both the heat shock protein (sHSP17.8) and StWRKY1 following pathogen invasion enables functioning of the latter to interact with the HSE present in the resistant StWRKY1 promoter region. PMC4765800.p004.s009: Silencing of the StWRKY1 gene was associated with signs of reduced late blight resistance by significantly increasing the pathogen biomass and decreasing the abundance of HCAAs. PMC4765800.p004.s010: This study provides convincing evidence on the role of StWRKY1 in the regulation of downstream genes to biosynthesize HCAAs, which are deposited to reinforce secondary cell walls.

events.event_label: Transcription factors affect secondary cell wall biosynthetic genes that participate in secondary cell wall biosynthesis

participants.label: biosynthesis of the secondary cell wall

events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p006.s006: Cell wall thickening reduces the spread of pathogens in plants beyond the site of infection, reducing lesion expansion. PMC4765800.p006.s007: The biosynthesis of the secondary cell wall is a developmental process that involves the co-ordinated expression of secondary cell wall biosynthetic genes regulated by a cascade of transcription factors. PMC4765800.p006.s008: Understanding such processes is vital for the manipulation of secondary cell wall thickening to resist pathogen development in plants.

events.event_label: HCAAs and related phenylpropanoid metabolites participate in the phenylpropanoid pathway and cell wall thickening
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p051.s001: Among the RRI metabolites identified, most of the high-fold-change metabolites belonged to the phenylpropanoid pathway, including the HCAAs N-feruloyltyramine (12.85), N-feruloyloctopamine (8.90), feruloylputrescine (5.03), and feruloylagmatine (5.01), which are known to be involved in cell wall thickening. PMC4765800.p051.s002: To confirm the deposition of HCAAs in cell walls, a histochemical staining technique was used to visualize the location of deposition of these metabolites. PMC4765800.p051.s003: Deposition of HCAAs (blue fluorescence intensity) was greater in pathogen-treated resistant than in mock-treated resistant and pathogen- or mock-treated susceptible genotypes (Supplementary Fig. S4 at JXB online). PMC4765800.p051.s004: The candidate RRI metabolites with the highest fold changes in abundance following pathogen inoculation were mapped onto their metabolic pathways (Fig. 2) to identify the catalytic enzymes, which were then searched in the Spud database ( accessed on 25 February 2015) to identify candidate RR genes.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 promotes HCAA accumulation and secondary cell wall thickening
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p084.s003: Concurrently, sHSP17.8 is also induced in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p084.s004: sHSP17.8 interacts with HSEs present in the StWRKY1 promoter region to enable functioning of StWRKY1. PMC4765800.p084.s005: Consequently, StWRKY1 protein interacts with HCAA biosynthetic genes 4-CL, TyDC, THT, and possibly others, inducing high-fold-change production of the HCAA metabolites N-feruloyltyramine, N-feruloyloctopamine, and others. PMC4765800.p084.s006: Taken together, these results suggest that StWRKY1 is required for the induction of HCAAs and defence by secondary cell wall thickening in potato against P. infestans. PMC4765800.p084.s007: These candidate genes can be used to replace non-functional genes in susceptible commercial cultivars, e.g. Shepody and Russet Burbank, based on genome-editing technologies to enhance quantitative resistance.

events.event_label: PAMPs activate MAPK cascade and transcription factors regulate resistance genes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p007.s006: PAMP also triggers phytohormones, such as jasmonic acid, salicylic acid, ethylene, abscisic acid, and gibberellins, to provide broad biochemical resistance and reduce pathogen growth. PMC4765800.p007.s007: These phytohormones fine-tune the pathways involved in the production of secondary metabolites that impart quantitative resistance. PMC4765800.p007.s008: Following recognition of PAMPs, the receptors trigger a phosphorylation cascade through the mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) signalling pathway, leading to the induction or repression of target genes. PMC4765800.p007.s009: Among the induced target genes are a variety of transcription factors that regulate plant defence responses involving up- or down-regulation of resistance-related (RR) genes by binding to specific DNA sequences in their promoter regions.

events.event_label: HSF induces sHSP17.8 which enhances transcriptional activity
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p057.s006: Interestingly, qRT-PCR revealed a higher fold change of 40.35 transcripts in the resistant (F06025) than in the susceptible (Shepody) genotype (Fig. 3B). PMC4765800.p057.s007: As HSPs are transcriptionally activated by heat shock factors (HSF) to interact with HSEs present on the promoter region of a gene, the possible regulation between sHSP17.8 and HSE was tested by in vivo LUC assay in potato protoplasts (Fig. 3C, D). PMC4765800.p057.s008: The StWRKY1 promoter from the resistant genotype had a relative LUC value of 42.77 as compared (P<0.001) with the susceptible genotype (10.25) and control vector (5.38). PMC4765800.p057.s009: Taken together, these results suggest that sHSP17.8 interacts with the HSE conserved domain present in the promoter region of StWRKY1 in the resistant genotype and is likely involved in mediating the high transcriptional activity of StWRKY1 proteins.

events.event_label: MAPK cascade involving StMEK1 and StWRKY1 activates RR genes in nucleus
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p076.s003: MAPK converts the signals generated from receptors to cellular responses. PMC4765800.p076.s004: Upon activation, MAPK translocates into the nucleus and phosphorylates the WRKY transcription factor, which in turn activates downstream gene expression. PMC4765800.p076.s005: In potato, StMEK1 was functionally characterised during defence reactions, which are also associated with StWRKY1, as was confirmed here by BiFC assay. PMC4765800.p076.s006: StMEK1 and StWRKY1 function together to induce the expression of downstream RR genes involved in plant defence.

events.event_label: late blight infection induces sHSP17.8 which interacts with and activates StWRKY1
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p078.s001: We detected significantly high fold changes of sHSP17.8 (fold change=40.35) in a resistant genotype of potato against late blight infection. PMC4765800.p078.s002: The presence of HSEs on the promoter region of the StWRKY1 gene in the resistant genotype, but not in the susceptible genotype (Supplementary Table S4), helped the sHSPs to interact and induce the transcription factor StWRKY1. PMC4765800.p078.s003: Small HSPs interact and provide stability to the proteins by acting as molecular chaperones. PMC4765800.p078.s004: LUC assay confirmed the transcriptional regulation of StWRKY1 by sHSP17.8.

events.event_label: RR genes encode HCAA biosynthetic enzymes and are regulated by transcription factors
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p051.s003: Deposition of HCAAs (blue fluorescence intensity) was greater in pathogen-treated resistant than in mock-treated resistant and pathogen- or mock-treated susceptible genotypes (Supplementary Fig. S4 at JXB online). PMC4765800.p051.s004: The candidate RRI metabolites with the highest fold changes in abundance following pathogen inoculation were mapped onto their metabolic pathways (Fig. 2) to identify the catalytic enzymes, which were then searched in the Spud database ( accessed on 25 February 2015) to identify candidate RR genes. PMC4765800.p051.s005: The RR genes that encoded the enzymes of these RRI metabolites were 4-coumarate:CoA ligase (4-CL) (GenBank accession number AF150686.1; chromosome 3), hydroxycinnamoyl transferase (HCT) (Gene ID: PGSC0003DMT400036695; chromosome 3), tyramine hydroxycinnamoyl transferase (THT) (GenBank accession number JX896425.1; chromosome 10), and tyrosine decarboxylase (TyDC) (Gene ID: PGSC0003DMT400038501; chromosome 3) (Fig. 2). PMC4765800.p051.s006: The expression of RR genes is transcriptionally regulated by the co-ordinated action of transcription factors which bind to cis-acting elements and regulate the expression of RR genes. PMC4765800.p051.s007: Previous studies reported that StWRKY1 (GenBank accession number AJ278507.1; chromosome 5) is induced and co-regulated with class I endochitinase during the compatible interaction of potato and P. infestans.

events.event_label: HCAAs participate in phenylpropanoid pathway, cell wall thickening and accumulate upon pathogen treatment
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p051.s001: Among the RRI metabolites identified, most of the high-fold-change metabolites belonged to the phenylpropanoid pathway, including the HCAAs N-feruloyltyramine (12.85), N-feruloyloctopamine (8.90), feruloylputrescine (5.03), and feruloylagmatine (5.01), which are known to be involved in cell wall thickening. PMC4765800.p051.s002: To confirm the deposition of HCAAs in cell walls, a histochemical staining technique was used to visualize the location of deposition of these metabolites. PMC4765800.p051.s003: Deposition of HCAAs (blue fluorescence intensity) was greater in pathogen-treated resistant than in mock-treated resistant and pathogen- or mock-treated susceptible genotypes (Supplementary Fig. S4 at JXB online). PMC4765800.p051.s004: The candidate RRI metabolites with the highest fold changes in abundance following pathogen inoculation were mapped onto their metabolic pathways (Fig. 2) to identify the catalytic enzymes, which were then searched in the Spud database ( accessed on 25 February 2015) to identify candidate RR genes.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 binds the THT promoter and regulates its expression
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p064.s006: EMSA results showed a shift in signal of biotinylated THT promoter that was inhibited in the presence of excess unlabelled competitor (Supplementary Fig. S6B). PMC4765800.p064.s007: These results indicate that StWRKY1 can specifically bind to the W1-box of the THT promoter and can regulate its gene expression.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 silencing increases lesion length and pathogen biomass
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p067.s001: To assess the effect of StWRKY1 in late blight resistance, the disease severity and biomass were quantified in silenced and non-silenced resistant plants. PMC4765800.p067.s002: The lesion length was higher (P<0.001) in silenced plants (26.19mm; AUDPC=80.68), significantly differing from the non-silenced plants (14.90mm; AUDPC=47.60). PMC4765800.p067.s003: Likewise, pathogen biomass was higher (P<0.001) in silenced plants (fold change=13.06) compared with non-silenced plants at 6 dpi (Fig. 6A, B). PMC4765800.p067.s004: Furthermore, the relative abundances of the transcripts of phenylpropanoid genes (4-CL, THT, TyDC, and HCT) were determined in silenced and non-silenced resistant plants with reference to the susceptible genotype (Fig. 6C–F).

events.event_label: StWRKY1 affects late blight resistance as measured by lesion length
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p068.s001: Effect of StWRKY1 silencing on late blight resistance mechanisms. PMC4765800.p068.s002: (A) Late blight disease severity progress. PMC4765800.p068.s003: Disease severity was quantified as lesion length (mm) at 3 d intervals. PMC4765800.p068.s004: (B) Biomass of P. infestans on silenced (S) and non-silenced (NS) resistant potato genotypes.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 binds RR gene promoters and activates HCAA biosynthesis
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p062.s003: The constructs were transformed into potato protoplasts. PMC4765800.p062.s004: We found that StWRKY1 bound to the promoter region of RR genes, and drastically activated LUC reporter expression in 4-CL (45.55) and THT (47.00) promoters compared with vector alone (4.16) (Fig. 5B), suggesting the transcriptional regulation of HCAA biosynthesis.

events.event_label: pathogen infection upregulates 4-CL, THT, TyDC genes for secondary cell wall reinforcement
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p080.s001: To combat pathogen infection, plants have developed specific metabolic pathways to biosynthesize metabolites that reinforce secondary cell walls as mechanical barriers. PMC4765800.p080.s002: Following pathogen inoculation, genes involved in the production of tyramine-derived HCAAs were 4-CL, THT, and TyDC, which were upregulated 2.13-, 2.98-, and 4.04-fold, respectively, in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p080.s003: Concurrently, metabolites produced from these candidate RRI genes, i.e. N-feruloyltyramine and N-feruloyloctopamine, were induced with significantly high fold changes in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p080.s004: The authors previous studies reported high-fold-change expression of THT and TyDC genes, along with high-fold-change accumulation of RRI metabolites N-feruloyltyramine, N-caffeoyltyramine, and N-feruloyloctopamine in leaves of resistant genotypes.

events.event_label: CaWRKY1a and CaWRKY1b are present in Coffea arabica and confer resistance to Hemileia vastatrix
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p075.s006: Likewise, the overexpression of CaWRKY27 in tobacco plants was associated with resistance to Ralstonia solanacearum infection through induction of jasmonic acid-, salicylic acid-, and ethylene-mediated signalling pathways. PMC4765800.p075.s007: Resistance to poplar canker was associated with overexpression of PtoWRKY60, which regulated high expression of defence-associated genes, such as PR5.1, PR5.2, PR5.4, PR5.5, and CPR5, in poplar. PMC4765800.p075.s008: Two CaWRKY1 genes, CaWRKY1a and CaWRKY1b (which bind to the pW1a promoter region), were identified in Coffea arabica and conferred resistance to the rust fungus Hemileia vastatrix. PMC4765800.p075.s009: These findings depict the involvement of the WRKY transcription factors in disease resistance, as described here in potato against P. infestans.

events.event_label: Phospholipids localize to membranes and participate in plant defence signalling pathways
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p059.s001: We detected a high fold change in abundance of induced phospholipid metabolites in the resistant genotype following pathogen infection [sphingolipids: (3′-sulfo)Galβ-Cer(d18:0/20:0(2OH)) (3.77), N-palmitoylsphingosine (2.90), and N-(eicosanoyl)-sphinganine (2.37); glycerophospholipids: 1-(9Z-nonadecenoyl)-glycero-3-phosphoserine (3.61), 1-hexadecyl-2-propionyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.50), and 1-octadecyl-2-ethyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.14)]. PMC4765800.p059.s002: These phospholipids are structural components of membranes and act as novel secondary messengers in plant defence signalling pathways. PMC4765800.p059.s003: These activate the MAPK cascade by triggering oxidative bursts. PMC4765800.p059.s004: MAPK (StMEK1) phosphorylates StWRKY1, which in turn activates defence gene expression.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 regulates phenylpropanoid metabolites contributing to late blight resistance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p001.s001: Transcription factor StWRKY1 regulates phenylpropanoid metabolites conferring late blight resistance in potato

events.event_label: pathogen induces StMEK1 activation via glycerophospholipids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p084.s001: Based on integrated metabolomics and genomics data, the authors propose a model to explain the role of StWRKY1, together with HCAA biosynthetic genes, in mediating potato secondary cell wall thickening (Fig. 7). PMC4765800.p084.s002: Following P. infestans invasion, a hierarchy of genes is induced in the phenylpropanoid pathway starting from the induction of phospholipid metabolites (glycerophospholipids and sphingolipids), possibly by enzymes produced by the pathogen, activating MAPK (StMEK1), which in turn phosphorylates the transcription factor (StWRKY1). PMC4765800.p084.s003: Concurrently, sHSP17.8 is also induced in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p084.s004: sHSP17.8 interacts with HSEs present in the StWRKY1 promoter region to enable functioning of StWRKY1.

events.event_label: Phenylpropanoid metabolites enhance cell wall thickening contributing to late blight resistance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p009.s003: The two genotypes with contrasting levels of resistance to Phytophthora infestans induced different metabolic pathways. PMC4765800.p009.s004: As reported by others, a high fold change in abundance of phenylpropanoid metabolites was observed in stems that appears to play a major role in late blight resistance by enhancing cell wall thickening through deposition of polyaromatic domains of suberin. PMC4765800.p009.s005: Phenylpropanoids were also observed in the current authors’ previous studies, but the molecular events in their biosynthesis and regulation were not revealed. PMC4765800.p009.s006: A hierarchy of genes involved in the induction of the phenylpropanoid pathway has been identified, although this is limited to model plants. PMC4765800.p009.s007: Therefore, candidate metabolites with high fold change in abundances were mapped onto metabolic pathways to identify their biosynthetic candidate genes based on genomic databases.

PMC4765800.p051.s002: To confirm the deposition of HCAAs in cell walls, a histochemical staining technique was used to visualize the location of deposition of these metabolites. PMC4765800.p051.s003: Deposition of HCAAs (blue fluorescence intensity) was greater in pathogen-treated resistant than in mock-treated resistant and pathogen- or mock-treated susceptible genotypes (Supplementary Fig. S4 at JXB online). events.event_label: RR metabolites detected in potato stems

PMC4765800.p089.s002: (B) RR metabolites identified in the resistant potato genotype inoculated with a mock-solution or P. infestans and their classification according to chemical groups.

events.event_label: Late blight occurs in potato and reduces its biomass
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p006.s001: Late blight caused by Phytophthora infestans (Mont.) de Bary is the most destructive disease of potato. PMC4765800.p006.s002: It reduces plant biomass or even may kill the plant, thus decreasing crop quantity and quality. PMC4765800.p006.s003: The resistance in potato can be improved by selecting resistance genes that are induced following pathogen invasion, either by molecular breeding or cis/trans genetic engineering. PMC4765800.p006.s004: Plant responses to stress are complex and involve numerous physiological, molecular, and cellular adaptations.

events.event_label: Phenylpropanoid metabolites detected in stems and enhance cell wall thickening
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p009.s003: The two genotypes with contrasting levels of resistance to Phytophthora infestans induced different metabolic pathways. PMC4765800.p009.s004: As reported by others, a high fold change in abundance of phenylpropanoid metabolites was observed in stems that appears to play a major role in late blight resistance by enhancing cell wall thickening through deposition of polyaromatic domains of suberin. PMC4765800.p009.s005: Phenylpropanoids were also observed in the current authors’ previous studies, but the molecular events in their biosynthesis and regulation were not revealed. PMC4765800.p009.s006: A hierarchy of genes involved in the induction of the phenylpropanoid pathway has been identified, although this is limited to model plants. PMC4765800.p009.s007: Therefore, candidate metabolites with high fold change in abundances were mapped onto metabolic pathways to identify their biosynthetic candidate genes based on genomic databases.

events.event_label: Lesion length correlates with disease severity (AUDPC) in late blight
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p042.s001: Late blight severity in the potato genotypes F06025 and Shepody was assessed by measuring lesion length over time following inoculation with P. infestans clonal lineage US-8. PMC4765800.p042.s002: The initial symptoms were observed as brown-coloured lesions on the inoculated stems at 3 dpi in both F06025 and Shepody. PMC4765800.p042.s003: By 9 dpi, the stem lesion length was 66.44mm (AUDPC=201.09) in Shepody, differing (P<0.001) from the resistant genotype F06025 with a lesion length of 13.44mm (AUDPC=49.71) (Fig. 1A and Supplementary Fig. S1 at JXB online).

events.event_label: CaWRKY1b confers resistance to Hemileia vastatrix in Coffea arabica
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p075.s006: Likewise, the overexpression of CaWRKY27 in tobacco plants was associated with resistance to Ralstonia solanacearum infection through induction of jasmonic acid-, salicylic acid-, and ethylene-mediated signalling pathways. PMC4765800.p075.s007: Resistance to poplar canker was associated with overexpression of PtoWRKY60, which regulated high expression of defence-associated genes, such as PR5.1, PR5.2, PR5.4, PR5.5, and CPR5, in poplar. PMC4765800.p075.s008: Two CaWRKY1 genes, CaWRKY1a and CaWRKY1b (which bind to the pW1a promoter region), were identified in Coffea arabica and conferred resistance to the rust fungus Hemileia vastatrix. PMC4765800.p075.s009: These findings depict the involvement of the WRKY transcription factors in disease resistance, as described here in potato against P. infestans.

events.event_label: HCAAs increase in pathogen-treated resistant plants and participate in phenylpropanoid pathway and cell wall thickening
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p051.s001: Among the RRI metabolites identified, most of the high-fold-change metabolites belonged to the phenylpropanoid pathway, including the HCAAs N-feruloyltyramine (12.85), N-feruloyloctopamine (8.90), feruloylputrescine (5.03), and feruloylagmatine (5.01), which are known to be involved in cell wall thickening. PMC4765800.p051.s002: To confirm the deposition of HCAAs in cell walls, a histochemical staining technique was used to visualize the location of deposition of these metabolites. PMC4765800.p051.s003: Deposition of HCAAs (blue fluorescence intensity) was greater in pathogen-treated resistant than in mock-treated resistant and pathogen- or mock-treated susceptible genotypes (Supplementary Fig. S4 at JXB online). PMC4765800.p051.s004: The candidate RRI metabolites with the highest fold changes in abundance following pathogen inoculation were mapped onto their metabolic pathways (Fig. 2) to identify the catalytic enzymes, which were then searched in the Spud database ( accessed on 25 February 2015) to identify candidate RR genes.

events.event_label: Silencing of StWRKY1 reduces late blight resistance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p067.s001: To assess the effect of StWRKY1 in late blight resistance, the disease severity and biomass were quantified in silenced and non-silenced resistant plants. PMC4765800.p067.s002: The lesion length was higher (P<0.001) in silenced plants (26.19mm; AUDPC=80.68), significantly differing from the non-silenced plants (14.90mm; AUDPC=47.60). PMC4765800.p067.s003: Likewise, pathogen biomass was higher (P<0.001) in silenced plants (fold change=13.06) compared with non-silenced plants at 6 dpi (Fig. 6A, B). PMC4765800.p067.s004: Furthermore, the relative abundances of the transcripts of phenylpropanoid genes (4-CL, THT, TyDC, and HCT) were determined in silenced and non-silenced resistant plants with reference to the susceptible genotype (Fig. 6C–F). PMC4765800.p067.s006: However, no significant reduction was observed in HCT transcripts in silenced plants (fold change=2.22) compared with resistant plants (2.41) and this was probably due to the absence of the W1-box in the HCT promoter (Fig. 6G and Supplementary Table S3). PMC4765800.p067.s007: We then tested the effect of StWRKY1 silencing on the production of HCAAs. PMC4765800.p067.s008: The RRI metabolites N-feruloyltyramine (fold change=10.13), N-feruloyloctopamine (6.65), feruloylputrescine (3.80), and feruloylagmatine (3.44) were reduced (P<0.05) after silencing (Table 2). PMC4765800.p067.s009: Taken together, these results provide compelling evidence that StWRKY1 is a positive regulator of late blight resistance through the reinforcement of secondary cell walls.

events.event_label: sHSP RSI2 and mitochondrial HSP22 are expressed in tomato
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p078.s006: The HSE present in the promoter region of the Arabidopsis APX1 gene acts as a functional component that helps to protect plants from oxidative stress. PMC4765800.p078.s007: In tobacco, the sHSP Ntshsp17 acts as a molecular chaperone and helps in the maintenance of cellular conditions suitable for inducing defence responses against bacterial wilt pathogen Ralstonia solanacearum by stabilizing signalling-related proteins. PMC4765800.p078.s008: In tomato, the sHSP RSI2 is a HSP20 member that specifically interacts and provides stability to the Fusarium oxysporum R protein I-2. PMC4765800.p078.s009: Likewise, the induction of mitochondrial HSP22 occurred in tomato during oxidative stress and helped to provide adoptive responses. PMC4765800.p078.s010: Conflicting with these results, wheat sHSP, Mds1 present on chromosome 3AS increases susceptibility to wheat gall midge and powdery mildew fungus Blumeria graminis f. sp. tritici.

events.event_label: Phospholipid metabolites activate MAPK cascade and oxidative bursts
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p076.s001: We also looked into the signalling pathways which involved induction of the WRKY transcription factor and detected high fold changes in several induced phospholipid metabolites in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p076.s002: These phospholipids act as novel secondary messengers in plant defence signalling pathways and activate the MAPK cascade by triggering oxidative bursts. PMC4765800.p076.s003: MAPK converts the signals generated from receptors to cellular responses. PMC4765800.p076.s004: Upon activation, MAPK translocates into the nucleus and phosphorylates the WRKY transcription factor, which in turn activates downstream gene expression.

events.event_label: HCAA metabolites accumulate in leaves of resistant genotype
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p080.s003: Concurrently, metabolites produced from these candidate RRI genes, i.e. N-feruloyltyramine and N-feruloyloctopamine, were induced with significantly high fold changes in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p080.s004: The authors previous studies reported high-fold-change expression of THT and TyDC genes, along with high-fold-change accumulation of RRI metabolites N-feruloyltyramine, N-caffeoyltyramine, and N-feruloyloctopamine in leaves of resistant genotypes. PMC4765800.p080.s005: However, here, for the first time, StWRKY1 was associated with the promoters of downstream HCAA biosynthetic genes 4-CL and THT in vivo and in vitro. PMC4765800.p080.s006: The results of the EMSA and LUC assays confirmed that StWRKY1 physically interacts with and induces high-fold-change expression of downstream genes involved in secondary cell wall thickening. PMC4765800.p080.s007: Likewise, the overexpression of VvWRKY1 induced a cinnamyl alcohol dehydrogenase and a caffeic acid O-methyl transferase gene in grape vines, providing higher resistance to downy mildew.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 interacts with HCAA biosynthetic genes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p084.s003: Concurrently, sHSP17.8 is also induced in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p084.s004: sHSP17.8 interacts with HSEs present in the StWRKY1 promoter region to enable functioning of StWRKY1. PMC4765800.p084.s005: Consequently, StWRKY1 protein interacts with HCAA biosynthetic genes 4-CL, TyDC, THT, and possibly others, inducing high-fold-change production of the HCAA metabolites N-feruloyltyramine, N-feruloyloctopamine, and others. PMC4765800.p084.s006: Taken together, these results suggest that StWRKY1 is required for the induction of HCAAs and defence by secondary cell wall thickening in potato against P. infestans. PMC4765800.p084.s007: These candidate genes can be used to replace non-functional genes in susceptible commercial cultivars, e.g. Shepody and Russet Burbank, based on genome-editing technologies to enhance quantitative resistance.

events.event_label: phenylpropanoid metabolites associates_with potato
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p001.s001: Transcription factor StWRKY1 regulates phenylpropanoid metabolites conferring late blight resistance in potato

events.event_label: Secondary cell wall thickening contributes_to Quantitative resistance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p004.s001: Quantitative resistance is polygenically controlled and durable, but the underlying molecular and biochemical mechanisms are poorly understood. PMC4765800.p004.s002: Secondary cell wall thickening is a critical process in quantitative resistance, regulated by transcriptional networks. PMC4765800.p004.s003: This paper provides compelling evidence on the functionality of StWRKY1 transcription factor, in a compatible interaction of potato–Phytophthora infestans, to extend our knowledge on the regulation of the metabolic pathway genes leading to strengthening the secondary cell wall. PMC4765800.p004.s004: A metabolomics approach was used to identify resistance-related metabolites belonging to the phenylpropanoid pathway and their biosynthetic genes regulated by StWRKY1.

events.event_label: pathogen activates_or_induces resistance genes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p006.s001: Late blight caused by Phytophthora infestans (Mont.) de Bary is the most destructive disease of potato. PMC4765800.p006.s002: It reduces plant biomass or even may kill the plant, thus decreasing crop quantity and quality. PMC4765800.p006.s003: The resistance in potato can be improved by selecting resistance genes that are induced following pathogen invasion, either by molecular breeding or cis/trans genetic engineering. PMC4765800.p006.s004: Plant responses to stress are complex and involve numerous physiological, molecular, and cellular adaptations.

events.event_label: Cell wall thickening reduces_or_impairs lesion expansion
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p006.s003: The resistance in potato can be improved by selecting resistance genes that are induced following pathogen invasion, either by molecular breeding or cis/trans genetic engineering. PMC4765800.p006.s004: Plant responses to stress are complex and involve numerous physiological, molecular, and cellular adaptations. PMC4765800.p006.s005: The plant cell wall is one of the barriers that pathogens need to overcome to successfully colonize plant tissues. PMC4765800.p006.s006: Cell wall thickening reduces the spread of pathogens in plants beyond the site of infection, reducing lesion expansion. PMC4765800.p006.s007: The biosynthesis of the secondary cell wall is a developmental process that involves the co-ordinated expression of secondary cell wall biosynthetic genes regulated by a cascade of transcription factors.

events.event_label: PAMPs activates_or_induces PTI
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p007.s001: Plants have evolved intricate strategies to recognize pathogen infection and devise effective defence responses. PMC4765800.p007.s002: The first line of defence following infection involves the recognition of pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs), which trigger basal levels of plant defence responses referred to as PAMP-triggered immunity (PTI). PMC4765800.p007.s003: To combat PTI, pathogens produce haustoria inside the plant cell to release effector proteins. PMC4765800.p007.s004: This invokes a second line of defence in plants to produce effector-specific resistance (R) proteins encoded by R genes.

events.event_label: R genes encodes effector-specific resistance (R) proteins
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p007.s003: To combat PTI, pathogens produce haustoria inside the plant cell to release effector proteins. PMC4765800.p007.s004: This invokes a second line of defence in plants to produce effector-specific resistance (R) proteins encoded by R genes. PMC4765800.p007.s005: The resulting hypersensitive reaction or effector-triggered immunity (ETI) constitutes qualitative resistance. PMC4765800.p007.s006: PAMP also triggers phytohormones, such as jasmonic acid, salicylic acid, ethylene, abscisic acid, and gibberellins, to provide broad biochemical resistance and reduce pathogen growth. PMC4765800.p007.s007: These phytohormones fine-tune the pathways involved in the production of secondary metabolites that impart quantitative resistance.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 contributes_to quantitative resistance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p009.s001: The present study was designed to elucidate the mechanisms by which StWRKY1 regulates the downstream genes leading to quantitative resistance to late blight in potato based on a metabolomics approach. PMC4765800.p009.s002: We identified high-fold-change RR metabolites in potato stems based on non-targeted metabolomics of two genotypes: a resistant (F06025) and a susceptible (Shepody) genotype. PMC4765800.p009.s003: The two genotypes with contrasting levels of resistance to Phytophthora infestans induced different metabolic pathways. PMC4765800.p009.s004: As reported by others, a high fold change in abundance of phenylpropanoid metabolites was observed in stems that appears to play a major role in late blight resistance by enhancing cell wall thickening through deposition of polyaromatic domains of suberin.

events.event_label: RR is_detected_in potato stems
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p009.s001: The present study was designed to elucidate the mechanisms by which StWRKY1 regulates the downstream genes leading to quantitative resistance to late blight in potato based on a metabolomics approach. PMC4765800.p009.s002: We identified high-fold-change RR metabolites in potato stems based on non-targeted metabolomics of two genotypes: a resistant (F06025) and a susceptible (Shepody) genotype. PMC4765800.p009.s003: The two genotypes with contrasting levels of resistance to Phytophthora infestans induced different metabolic pathways. PMC4765800.p009.s004: As reported by others, a high fold change in abundance of phenylpropanoid metabolites was observed in stems that appears to play a major role in late blight resistance by enhancing cell wall thickening through deposition of polyaromatic domains of suberin.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 is_detected_in potato
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p009.s006: A hierarchy of genes involved in the induction of the phenylpropanoid pathway has been identified, although this is limited to model plants. PMC4765800.p009.s007: Therefore, candidate metabolites with high fold change in abundances were mapped onto metabolic pathways to identify their biosynthetic candidate genes based on genomic databases. PMC4765800.p009.s008: WRKYs are implicated in the regulation of plant defence against pathogens, but only a few members of the WRKY superfamily in potato have been isolated or functionally characterized. PMC4765800.p009.s009: StWRKY1 was reported in potato following infection with P. infestans, but no details on metabolic pathway regulation were revealed. PMC4765800.p009.s010: The current study uses a forward genetics approach to discover StWRKY1 from the high fold change in abundance of hydroxycinnamic acid amides (HCAAs) detected based on metabolic profiling, and reverse genetics to validate in depth the transcriptional activation of StWRKY1, possible interactions between StWRKY1 and HCAA biosynthetic genes, and their link to secondary cell wall strengthening to contain pathogen spread following P. infestans invasion.

events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p073.s001: In the present study, an integrated metabolomics, gene expression, and gene sequencing information approach was used to discover and explore the role of transcription factor StWRKY1, and also to explain the late blight resistance mechanisms in potato genotypes F06025 and Shepody. PMC4765800.p073.s002: These genotypes varied in their resistance levels and also in accumulation of metabolites. PMC4765800.p073.s003: Most of the high-fold-change metabolites identified in the resistant genotype belonged to the phenylpropanoid pathway, especially HCAAs. PMC4765800.p073.s004: These metabolites are well known for their role in cell wall thickening, forming antimicrobial barriers.

events.event_label: cell wall thickening reduces_or_impairs lesion expansion
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p073.s003: Most of the high-fold-change metabolites identified in the resistant genotype belonged to the phenylpropanoid pathway, especially HCAAs. PMC4765800.p073.s004: These metabolites are well known for their role in cell wall thickening, forming antimicrobial barriers. PMC4765800.p073.s005: Plants have developed a system to maintain cell wall integrity by activation of signalling molecules, transcriptional reprogramming and biosynthesis of defence metabolites that limit pathogen infection. PMC4765800.p073.s006: Cell wall thickening reduces the spread of pathogens in plants beyond the site of infection, reducing lesion expansion. PMC4765800.p073.s007: These complex molecules are not generally broken down by plant pathogens.

events.event_label: StWRKY1 contributes_to secondary cell wall thickening
events.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p084.s001: Based on integrated metabolomics and genomics data, the authors propose a model to explain the role of StWRKY1, together with HCAA biosynthetic genes, in mediating potato secondary cell wall thickening (Fig. 7). PMC4765800.p084.s002: Following P. infestans invasion, a hierarchy of genes is induced in the phenylpropanoid pathway starting from the induction of phospholipid metabolites (glycerophospholipids and sphingolipids), possibly by enzymes produced by the pathogen, activating MAPK (StMEK1), which in turn phosphorylates the transcription factor (StWRKY1). PMC4765800.p084.s003: Concurrently, sHSP17.8 is also induced in the resistant genotype. PMC4765800.p084.s004: sHSP17.8 interacts with HSEs present in the StWRKY1 promoter region to enable functioning of StWRKY1.

PMC4765800.p004.s003: This paper provides compelling evidence on the functionality of StWRKY1 transcription factor, in a compatible interaction of potato–Phytophthora infestans, to extend our knowledge on the regulation of the metabolic pathway genes leading to strengthening the secondary cell wall. relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p004.s006: Sequencing of the promoter region of StWRKY1 in susceptible genotypes revealed the absence of heat shock elements (HSEs). PMC4765800.p004.s007: Simultaneous induction of both the heat shock protein (sHSP17.8) and StWRKY1 following pathogen invasion enables functioning of the latter to interact with the HSE present in the resistant StWRKY1 promoter region. PMC4765800.p004.s008: EMSA and luciferase transient expression assays further revealed direct binding of StWRKY1 to promoters of hydroxycinnamic acid amide (HCAA) biosynthetic genes encoding 4-coumarate:CoA ligase and tyramine hydroxycinnamoyl transferase. PMC4765800.p004.s009: Silencing of the StWRKY1 gene was associated with signs of reduced late blight resistance by significantly increasing the pathogen biomass and decreasing the abundance of HCAAs. PMC4765800.p004.s010: This study provides convincing evidence on the role of StWRKY1 in the regulation of downstream genes to biosynthesize HCAAs, which are deposited to reinforce secondary cell walls.

relations.subject: Late blight

evidence.text: Late blight caused by Phytophthora infestans (Mont.) de Bary is the most destructive disease of potato.

relations.evidence_preview: Late blight caused by Phytophthora infestans (Mont.) de Bary is the most destructive disease of potato.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p006.s001: Late blight caused by Phytophthora infestans (Mont.) de Bary is the most destructive disease of potato. PMC4765800.p006.s002: It reduces plant biomass or even may kill the plant, thus decreasing crop quantity and quality. PMC4765800.p006.s003: The resistance in potato can be improved by selecting resistance genes that are induced following pathogen invasion, either by molecular breeding or cis/trans genetic engineering. PMC4765800.p006.s004: Plant responses to stress are complex and involve numerous physiological, molecular, and cellular adaptations.

relations.subject: PAMPs

evidence.text: The first line of defence following infection involves the recognition of pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs), which trigger basal levels of plant defence responses referred to as PAMP-triggered immunity (PTI).

relations.evidence_preview: The first line of defence following infection involves the recognition of pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs), which trigger basal levels of plant defence responses referred to as PAMP-triggered immunity (PTI).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p007.s001: Plants have evolved intricate strategies to recognize pathogen infection and devise effective defence responses. PMC4765800.p007.s002: The first line of defence following infection involves the recognition of pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs), which trigger basal levels of plant defence responses referred to as PAMP-triggered immunity (PTI). PMC4765800.p007.s003: To combat PTI, pathogens produce haustoria inside the plant cell to release effector proteins. PMC4765800.p007.s004: This invokes a second line of defence in plants to produce effector-specific resistance (R) proteins encoded by R genes.

relations.triple: R genes encodes effector-specific resistance (R) proteins
relations.subject: R genes

evidence.text: This invokes a second line of defence in plants to produce effector-specific resistance (R) proteins encoded by R genes.

relations.evidence_preview: This invokes a second line of defence in plants to produce effector-specific resistance (R) proteins encoded by R genes.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p007.s003: To combat PTI, pathogens produce haustoria inside the plant cell to release effector proteins. PMC4765800.p007.s004: This invokes a second line of defence in plants to produce effector-specific resistance (R) proteins encoded by R genes. PMC4765800.p007.s005: The resulting hypersensitive reaction or effector-triggered immunity (ETI) constitutes qualitative resistance. PMC4765800.p007.s006: PAMP also triggers phytohormones, such as jasmonic acid, salicylic acid, ethylene, abscisic acid, and gibberellins, to provide broad biochemical resistance and reduce pathogen growth. PMC4765800.p007.s007: These phytohormones fine-tune the pathways involved in the production of secondary metabolites that impart quantitative resistance.

relations.triple: WRKY transcription factor confers_tolerance_to pathogen infection
relations.subject: WRKY transcription factor

evidence.text: Overexpression of WRKY transcription factor increased tolerance to pathogen infection through induction of phytohormone-mediated signalling pathways in grapes and tobacco.

relations.evidence_preview: Overexpression of WRKY transcription factor increased tolerance to pathogen infection through induction of phytohormone-mediated signalling pathways in grapes and tobacco.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p008.s001: WRKY domain-containing proteins are one of the largest families of transcriptional regulators in plants. PMC4765800.p008.s002: They have a DNA-binding domain called a W-box (TTGACC/T or WRKYGQK) and are involved in plant immune responses. PMC4765800.p008.s003: Overexpression of WRKY transcription factor increased tolerance to pathogen infection through induction of phytohormone-mediated signalling pathways in grapes and tobacco. PMC4765800.p008.s004: In contrast, the overexpression of WRKY reduced resistance mechanisms in rice and Capsicum plants.

relations.subject: StWRKY1

evidence.text: The present study was designed to elucidate the mechanisms by which StWRKY1 regulates the downstream genes leading to quantitative resistance to late blight in potato based on a metabolomics approach.

relations.evidence_preview: The present study was designed to elucidate the mechanisms by which StWRKY1 regulates the downstream genes leading to quantitative resistance to late blight in potato based on a metabolomics approach.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p009.s001: The present study was designed to elucidate the mechanisms by which StWRKY1 regulates the downstream genes leading to quantitative resistance to late blight in potato based on a metabolomics approach. PMC4765800.p009.s002: We identified high-fold-change RR metabolites in potato stems based on non-targeted metabolomics of two genotypes: a resistant (F06025) and a susceptible (Shepody) genotype. PMC4765800.p009.s003: The two genotypes with contrasting levels of resistance to Phytophthora infestans induced different metabolic pathways. PMC4765800.p009.s004: As reported by others, a high fold change in abundance of phenylpropanoid metabolites was observed in stems that appears to play a major role in late blight resistance by enhancing cell wall thickening through deposition of polyaromatic domains of suberin.

PMC4765800.p009.s005: Phenylpropanoids were also observed in the current authors’ previous studies, but the molecular events in their biosynthesis and regulation were not revealed. PMC4765800.p009.s006: A hierarchy of genes involved in the induction of the phenylpropanoid pathway has been identified, although this is limited to model plants. PMC4765800.p009.s007: Therefore, candidate metabolites with high fold change in abundances were mapped onto metabolic pathways to identify their biosynthetic candidate genes based on genomic databases.

PMC4765800.p009.s010: The current study uses a forward genetics approach to discover StWRKY1 from the high fold change in abundance of hydroxycinnamic acid amides (HCAAs) detected based on metabolic profiling, and reverse genetics to validate in depth the transcriptional activation of StWRKY1, possible interactions between StWRKY1 and HCAA biosynthetic genes, and their link to secondary cell wall strengthening to contain pathogen spread following P. infestans invasion.

relations.subject: Late blight

evidence.text: Late blight severity in the potato genotypes F06025 and Shepody was assessed by measuring lesion length over time following inoculation with P. infestans clonal lineage US-8.

relations.subject: lesion length

evidence.text: By 9 dpi, the stem lesion length was 66.44mm (AUDPC=201.09) in Shepody, differing (P<0.001) from the resistant genotype F06025 with a lesion length of 13.44mm (AUDPC=49.71) (Fig. 1A and Supplementary Fig. S1 at JXB online).

relations.subject: HCAAs

evidence.text: Among the RRI metabolites identified, most of the high-fold-change metabolites belonged to the phenylpropanoid pathway, including the HCAAs N-feruloyltyramine (12.85), N-feruloyloctopamine (8.90), feruloylputrescine (5.03), and feruloylagmatine (5.01), which are known to be involved in cell wall thickening.

relations.evidence_preview: Among the RRI metabolites identified, most of the high-fold-change metabolites belonged to the phenylpropanoid pathway, including the HCAAs N-feruloyltyramine (12.85), N-feruloyloctopamine (8.90), feruloylputrescine (5.03), and feruloylagmatine (5.01), which are known to be involved in cell wall thickening.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p051.s001: Among the RRI metabolites identified, most of the high-fold-change metabolites belonged to the phenylpropanoid pathway, including the HCAAs N-feruloyltyramine (12.85), N-feruloyloctopamine (8.90), feruloylputrescine (5.03), and feruloylagmatine (5.01), which are known to be involved in cell wall thickening. PMC4765800.p051.s002: To confirm the deposition of HCAAs in cell walls, a histochemical staining technique was used to visualize the location of deposition of these metabolites. PMC4765800.p051.s003: Deposition of HCAAs (blue fluorescence intensity) was greater in pathogen-treated resistant than in mock-treated resistant and pathogen- or mock-treated susceptible genotypes (Supplementary Fig. S4 at JXB online). PMC4765800.p051.s004: The candidate RRI metabolites with the highest fold changes in abundance following pathogen inoculation were mapped onto their metabolic pathways (Fig. 2) to identify the catalytic enzymes, which were then searched in the Spud database ( accessed on 25 February 2015) to identify candidate RR genes.

relations.subject: RR genes

evidence.text: The RR genes that encoded the enzymes of these RRI metabolites were 4-coumarate:CoA ligase (4-CL) (GenBank accession number AF150686.1; chromosome 3), hydroxycinnamoyl transferase (HCT) (Gene ID: PGSC0003DMT400036695; chromosome 3), tyramine hydroxycinnamoyl transferase (THT) (GenBank accession number JX896425.1; chromosome 10), and tyrosine decarboxylase (TyDC) (Gene ID: PGSC0003DMT400038501; chromosome 3) (Fig. 2).

PMC4765800.p051.s005: The RR genes that encoded the enzymes of these RRI metabolites were 4-coumarate:CoA ligase (4-CL) (GenBank accession number AF150686.1; chromosome 3), hydroxycinnamoyl transferase (HCT) (Gene ID: PGSC0003DMT400036695; chromosome 3), tyramine hydroxycinnamoyl transferase (THT) (GenBank accession number JX896425.1; chromosome 10), and tyrosine decarboxylase (TyDC) (Gene ID: PGSC0003DMT400038501; chromosome 3) (Fig. 2). PMC4765800.p051.s006: The expression of RR genes is transcriptionally regulated by the co-ordinated action of transcription factors which bind to cis-acting elements and regulate the expression of RR genes. PMC4765800.p051.s007: Previous studies reported that StWRKY1 (GenBank accession number AJ278507.1; chromosome 5) is induced and co-regulated with class I endochitinase during the compatible interaction of potato and P. infestans.

relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p055.s001: Relative transcript expression and regulation of StWRKY1 by sHSP17.8. PMC4765800.p055.s002: The relative transcript expression for (A) StWRKY1 and (B) sHSP17.8 was confirmed in the resistant genotype relative to the susceptible genotype following P. infestans and mock-solution inoculation at 48 hpi using qRT-PCR in comparison with reference genes for elongation factor-1α and β-tubulin. PMC4765800.p055.s003: See main text for further details. PMC4765800.p055.s004: Regulation of StWRKY1 by sHSP17.8 was confirmed by LUC transient expression assay: (C) constructs used and (D) relative LUC reporter activity by HSP17.8.

relations.subject: HSF

evidence.text: As HSPs are transcriptionally activated by heat shock factors (HSF) to interact with HSEs present on the promoter region of a gene, the possible regulation between sHSP17.8 and HSE was tested by in vivo LUC assay in potato protoplasts (Fig. 3C, D).

relations.evidence_preview: As HSPs are transcriptionally activated by heat shock factors (HSF) to interact with HSEs present on the promoter region of a gene, the possible regulation between sHSP17.8 and HSE was tested by in vivo LUC assay in potato protoplasts (Fig. 3C, D).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p057.s006: Interestingly, qRT-PCR revealed a higher fold change of 40.35 transcripts in the resistant (F06025) than in the susceptible (Shepody) genotype (Fig. 3B). PMC4765800.p057.s007: As HSPs are transcriptionally activated by heat shock factors (HSF) to interact with HSEs present on the promoter region of a gene, the possible regulation between sHSP17.8 and HSE was tested by in vivo LUC assay in potato protoplasts (Fig. 3C, D). PMC4765800.p057.s008: The StWRKY1 promoter from the resistant genotype had a relative LUC value of 42.77 as compared (P<0.001) with the susceptible genotype (10.25) and control vector (5.38). PMC4765800.p057.s009: Taken together, these results suggest that sHSP17.8 interacts with the HSE conserved domain present in the promoter region of StWRKY1 in the resistant genotype and is likely involved in mediating the high transcriptional activity of StWRKY1 proteins.

relations.subject: pathogen infection

evidence.text: We detected a high fold change in abundance of induced phospholipid metabolites in the resistant genotype following pathogen infection [sphingolipids: (3′-sulfo)Galβ-Cer(d18:0/20:0(2OH)) (3.77), N-palmitoylsphingosine (2.90), and N-(eicosanoyl)-sphinganine (2.37); glycerophospholipids: 1-(9Z-nonadecenoyl)-glycero-3-phosphoserine (3.61), 1-hexadecyl-2-propionyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.50), and 1-octadecyl-2-ethyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.14)].

relations.evidence_preview: We detected a high fold change in abundance of induced phospholipid metabolites in the resistant genotype following pathogen infection [sphingolipids: (3′-sulfo)Galβ-Cer(d18:0/20:0(2OH)) (3.77), N-palmitoylsphingosine (2.90), and N-(eicosanoyl)-sphinganine (2.37); glycerophospholipids: 1-(9Z-nonadecenoyl)-glycero-3-phosphoserine (3.61), 1-hexadecyl-2-propionyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.50), and 1-octadecyl-2-ethyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.14)].
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p059.s001: We detected a high fold change in abundance of induced phospholipid metabolites in the resistant genotype following pathogen infection [sphingolipids: (3′-sulfo)Galβ-Cer(d18:0/20:0(2OH)) (3.77), N-palmitoylsphingosine (2.90), and N-(eicosanoyl)-sphinganine (2.37); glycerophospholipids: 1-(9Z-nonadecenoyl)-glycero-3-phosphoserine (3.61), 1-hexadecyl-2-propionyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.50), and 1-octadecyl-2-ethyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (3.14)]. PMC4765800.p059.s002: These phospholipids are structural components of membranes and act as novel secondary messengers in plant defence signalling pathways. PMC4765800.p059.s003: These activate the MAPK cascade by triggering oxidative bursts. PMC4765800.p059.s004: MAPK (StMEK1) phosphorylates StWRKY1, which in turn activates defence gene expression.

evidence.text: We found that StWRKY1 bound to the promoter region of RR genes, and drastically activated LUC reporter expression in 4-CL (45.55) and THT (47.00) promoters compared with vector alone (4.16) (Fig. 5B), suggesting the transcriptional regulation of HCAA biosynthesis.

relations.triple: HCAAs affects secondary cell wall thickening
relations.subject: HCAAs

evidence.text: The HCAAs, including N-feruloyltyramine, N-feruloyloctopamine, feruloylagmatine, and feruloylputrescine, play a critical role in secondary cell wall thickening.

relations.evidence_preview: The HCAAs, including N-feruloyltyramine, N-feruloyloctopamine, feruloylagmatine, and feruloylputrescine, play a critical role in secondary cell wall thickening.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p064.s001: The HCAAs, including N-feruloyltyramine, N-feruloyloctopamine, feruloylagmatine, and feruloylputrescine, play a critical role in secondary cell wall thickening. PMC4765800.p064.s002: Accordingly, the physical interaction of StWRKY1 with HCAA biosynthetic gene THT was tested by in vitro EMSA as additional evidence (Supplementary Fig. S6 at JXB online). PMC4765800.p064.s003: For EMSA, the purified recombinant StWRKY1 protein fractions had a molecular weight of 25kDa (Supplementary Fig. S6A) as analysed on 12% SDS-PAGE. PMC4765800.p064.s004: This was in agreement with the theoretical molecular weight of 23kDa (19.86kDa for the native protein plus 3kDa for the His-tag).

relations.subject: StWRKY1

evidence.text: These results indicate that StWRKY1 can specifically bind to the W1-box of the THT promoter and can regulate its gene expression.

relations.evidence_preview: These results indicate that StWRKY1 can specifically bind to the W1-box of the THT promoter and can regulate its gene expression.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC4765800.p064.s006: EMSA results showed a shift in signal of biotinylated THT promoter that was inhibited in the presence of excess unlabelled competitor (Supplementary Fig. S6B). PMC4765800.p064.s007: These results indicate that StWRKY1 can specifically bind to the W1-box of the THT promoter and can regulate its gene expression.
